# Contributing to my projects

Whether you're here to squash bugs, build new features, or make the documentation shine — you're exactly who I've been hoping for.

I'll get you from "I want to help!" to "Look what we built!" as smoothly as possible. Don't worry if you're new to this — I've been there too, and I'm here to help every step of the way!

## 🚀 Let's get set up

### Getting repository access

Fork the repository you want to work on — it's like getting your own copy to play with! No pressure, just make it cool and then show me what you've built.

### Setting up your development playground

Ready to dive in? Here's your quick setup checklist:

1. **Grab the code** — Clone the repository (or your shiny new fork)
2. **Get the dependencies** — Follow the `README.md` instructions (I promise, they're not scary)
3. **Create your feature branch** — Use a descriptive name like `feature/user-auth` or `fix/login-bug`
4. **Set up your environment** — Copy any `.env.example` files and install the tools you need

## 🤝 Ways to make my projects better

### Found a bug? Let's squash it together

Nobody likes bugs (except maybe entomologists), so I'm always grateful when you find them! When reporting bugs, help me help you by giving me:

- A clear, descriptive title (like "Login button disappears when I close my eyes")
- Step-by-step instructions to reproduce the issue
- Your environment details (OS, browser, etc.)

> [!TIP]
> Take a peek at existing issues first — someone might've already spotted the same bug!

### Got a new idea? I'm all ears

Before diving into code, create an issue to tell me about:

- The problem you're trying to solve (what's driving you crazy?)
- Your proposed solution (how would you fix it?)
- Who else might benefit from this change?
- Any alternative approaches you've considered.

I love discussing ideas before implementation — it saves everyone time and often leads to better solutions!

### Ready to write code? Let's do this

When you're coding, keep these guidelines in mind:

- **Stay focused** — One issue or feature per contribution keeps things clean.
- **Follow the patterns** — When in doubt, check existing code for style cues and match what's already there.
- **Write code that tells a story** — Clear, self-documenting code is a gift to your future self.
- **Update the docs** — Future contributors will thank you for keeping documentation current.
- **Make sure tests pass** — Green lights all around make everyone happy.

## 🔄 The pull request dance

Ready to share your work with the world? Here's how to make your pull request shine:

1. **Create your pull request** — Use the template and tell me about the cool things you've built.
2. **Connect the dots** — Link related issues with "Fixes #123" or "Closes #456". (GitHub loves this!)
3. **Embrace feedback** — Reviews are collaborative, not combative. I'm trying to make things better together with you.
4. **Keep things fresh** — Rebase or merge to stay current with the main branch.

Don't worry — any automated tests will check your work, and I'll provide helpful feedback to ensure everything meets the project standards. Think of it as a friendly code review, not a judgment!

## 📏 Keeping things tidy

### Style and quality (or: how to make code that doesn't make me cry)

- **Follow the vibe** — Each project has its own style. When in doubt, look around and match what's already there. It's like dressing for the occasion!
- **Use the tools** — Automated formatters are your friends. They handle the boring stuff so you can focus on coding.
- **Name things well** — `getUserById` is much more helpful than `func1`. Your future self (and everyone else) will appreciate descriptive names.

### Commit messages that tell a story

I use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) — think of them as tiny headlines for your changes:

```text
type(scope): description

Examples:
feat(auth): add two-factor authentication
fix(api): resolve memory leak in sessions
docs(readme): update installation steps
```

Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `style`, `refactor`, `test`, and `chore`. Don't stress too much about getting it perfect — I'm more interested in clear communication than rigid formatting!

### Testing (since nobody likes surprises in production)

- **Write tests for new features** — You'll be grateful when changes don't break everything.
- **Make sure existing tests still pass** — Green is good, red means we need to talk.
- **Focus on the important stuff** — Test critical functionality and those sneaky edge cases that always cause trouble.

## ❓ Stuck? I've got your back

Don't be shy about asking for help — I'm friendly and remember what it was like when I was starting out!

- **Found a bug or technical issue?** Create a detailed bug report and I'll investigate it.
- **Have general questions?** Use the "❓ Question & support" issue template — no question is too basic!
- **Want to discuss a feature idea?** Start with a feature request issue and let's brainstorm.
- **Struggling with setup?** Check the `README.md` first, but if you're stuck, ask in an issue. I'd rather help you than have you give up!

> [!NOTE]
> Security issues get VIP treatment through the [Security Policy](SECURITY.md) — they jump to the front of my priority list.

## 🌍 Community vibes

I'm here because I love building stuff, and I want everyone to feel welcome and included. All contributors follow the [Code of Conduct](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) — basically, just be nice to each other!

The goal is simple: create an environment where everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas, asking questions, and learning from each other.

---

_Still have questions? I'm just an email away at [william@hallin.media](mailto:william@hallin.media) — don't be a stranger!_
